coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional magnetic carbon dots for the rapid fluorescent detection and the efficient adsorptive removal of copper ion

coredata.dc:description: Developing a bifunctional material capable of both sensing and removing heavy metal ions is crucial but remains a significant challenge. Herein, magnetic carbon dots (M/CDs) were developed using ferroferric oxide (Fe3O4) and riboflavin (C17H20N4O6). M/CDs with optimal fluorescence properties were obtained under the specific preparation conditions of a molar ratio of riboflavin / Fe3O4 of 6:5, at a temperature of 200 °C and following a reaction time of 24 h. The structure of M/CDs was characterized using TEM, XRD, FTIR and VSM, which exhibited the prepared M/CDs contained high-affinity groups (–CN, C=O) and excellent magnetic separation properties (83.87 emu/g), offering selective detection and higher separation capacity for copper ion (Cu2+). The fluorescence intensity of the M/CDs progressively decreased with increasing concentrations of Cu2+, and the calculated detection limit was determined to be 0.096 mg/L, which was below the permissible limit. Moreover, the M/CDs demonstrated a removal efficiency of 89 % for 5 mg/L of Cu2+ across a broad pH range. The adsorption data were well described by the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Freundlich isotherm models. Notably, the reusability could be achieved by recycling at least five turns by using an external magnet. All of these indicate magnetic carbon dots hold great promise for simultaneous detection and removal of Cu2+, offering a feasible solution for assessing water quality and treating wastewater.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional magnetic carbon dots for the rapid fluorescent detection and the efficient adsorptive removal of copper ion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a bifunctional material capable of both sensing and removing heavy metal ions is crucial but remains a significant challenge. Herein, magnetic carbon dots (M/CDs) were developed using ferroferric oxide (Fe3O4) and riboflavin (C17H20N4O6). M/CDs with optimal fluorescence properties were obtained under the specific preparation conditions of a molar ratio of riboflavin / Fe3O4 of 6:5, at a temperature of 200 °C and following a reaction time of 24 h. The structure of M/CDs was characterized using TEM, XRD, FTIR and VSM, which exhibited the prepared M/CDs contained high-affinity groups (–CN, C=O) and excellent magnetic separation properties (83.87 emu/g), offering selective detection and higher separation capacity for copper ion (Cu2+). The fluorescence intensity of the M/CDs progressively decreased with increasing concentrations of Cu2+, and the calculated detection limit was determined to be 0.096 mg/L, which was below the permissible limit. Moreover, the M/CDs demonstrated a removal efficiency of 89 % for 5 mg/L of Cu2+ across a broad pH range. The adsorption data were well described by the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Freundlich isotherm models. Notably, the reusability could be achieved by recycling at least five turns by using an external magnet. All of these indicate magnetic carbon dots hold great promise for simultaneous detection and removal of Cu2+, offering a feasible solution for assessing water quality and treating wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ions (Cu2+) are essential metal ions for humans, playing a critical role in a multitude of life processes [1,2]. They are indispensable in many enzymes and actively participate in metabolic activities [3]. However, an excessive accumulation of Cu2+ in the human body can result in a range of health issues [4,5], including Parkinson, heart disease, joint pain, and gastrointestinal disorders [6]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has set a strict limit of 1.3 mg/L for the concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water [7]. Consequently, there is an urgent requirement for the advancement of selective methods that can both detect and remove Cu2+ [8].

Currently, the methods employed for the detection of Cu2+ primarily include inductively coupled plasma (ICP) [9], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [10], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (XRF) [11], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12] and so on. Although these methods offer significant advantages in terms of accuracy and sensitivity, their high cost and time-consuming nature make them difficult to implement on a routine basis, requiring professional operation [13,14]. In contrast, the use of fluorescence probes in analytical methods addresses these limitations due to their ease of sample operation, exceptional stability, and excellent selectivity [15,16]. Accordingly, there has been a growing interest in employing fluorescent probes for the detection and monitoring of Cu2+. In addition, a range of strategies, including ion exchange [17], membrane technology [18], co-precipitation [19], biological treatment [20], and others, have shown effective capabilities in the removal of heavy metal ions [21]. Among the many methods, adsorption is widely used for Cu2+ removal on due to its efficiency, simplicity, reusability, and eco-friendliness [22,23]. However, despite the significant achievements in the separate detection and removal of Cu2+, materials that can serve both as detection probes and adsorbents are not yet available [24]. Thus, the development of multifunctional materials capable of rapidly detecting and removing Cu2+ simultaneously is crucial for the effective treatment of environmental pollution.

To date, carbon dots (CDs) have been recognized for their numerous superior properties, such as low toxicity, high quantum fluorescence yield, dispersibility, bio-degradation, and biocompatibility [25–28]. A variety of CDs have been developed and successfully utilized in the areas of detection [29–32]. Zhao et al. prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for the detection of Cu2+ in water, fruit juice, and urine sample [33]. Ge’s group also designed fluorescent N-CDs for detecting both Cu2+ and S2- [34]. Although CDs have the potential to serve as fluorescent sensors for detecting pollutants, additional research and development are necessary to fully harness their capabilities. The task of effectively collecting unwanted Cu2+ from water resources is a significant challenge that demands considerable effort, especially when compared to single-function detection systems. Feasibly, by endowing CDs with magnetic properties, they can exhibit magnetic collection behavior, which not only enhances the detection of heavy metal ions by CDs but also resolves the issues of poor recovery and solubility when CDs are used as adsorbents in water. Notably, the incorporation of magnetic nature metal ions into CDs has a beneficial effect on their optical properties through the introduction of heteroatoms, offering new perspectives for the design of dual-functional probes [35]. And both Fe3O4 and CDs are recognized as environmentally benign substances [36]. Their integration is a synergistic combination that creates essential functions for detection and separation. Moreover, relatively few reports on the simultaneous specific identification and removal of Cu2+ using CDs-based materials.

In this work, a bifunctional magnetic carbon dots composite (M/CDs) was designed via a hydrothermal method, aimed at the detection and separation of Cu2+ (Fig. S1). The molar ratio of raw material, temperature, and time were optimized to achieve the highest fluorescence intensity and maximum removal efficiency. Then, the surface morphology, magnetic separation properties, chemical functional group, and detection mechanism of M/CDs were comprehensively investigated. Furthermore, the strong fluorescence emitted by M/CDs was utilized to quantify the concentration of Cu2+ and to investigate its adsorption behavior towards Cu2+.
Detailed information was placed in supporting material.

Initially, Fe3O4 was prepared using a method that had been previously reported [37]. Subsequently, the (−)-Riboflavin (0.228 g, 0.6 mmol) and the as-prepared Fe3O4 (0.116 g, 0.5 mmol) were fully dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water and then subjected to ultrasound treatment for 1 h. Afterwards, the homogeneous solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and then heated at 200 °C for 24 h. Finally, after the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to separate the supernatant and the solid phase. The solid phase s was then repeatedly washed with distilled water and dried. The supernatant and powder were collected for subsequent use. Additionally, the molar ratio of (−)-riboflavin to Fe3O4, the temperature, and the reaction time were optimized to determine the optimal preparation conditions.

In the Cu2+ detection assay, 300 μL of the supernatant from the M/CDs was combined with different concentrations of Cu2+ standard solutions and HEPES buffer for evaluation. The total volume of the detection system was adjusted to 4 mL. After incubation for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system was measured at an excitation wavelength of 468 nm. The selectivity of M/CDs for the target analyte was evaluated by separately adding 20 mg/L of Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cl-, HCO3 – and other ions to the aforementioned probe system. To investigate the anti-interference capability of M/CDs, 20 mg/L of Cu2+ and 100 mg/L of the each previously mentioned interfering ion were incorporated into the detection system to determine their effects on fluorescence intensity. To assess the efficiency and precision of the M/CDs, real water samples were tested, including tap water (pH=7.80) from Yunnan Minzu University and river water (pH=7.86) collected near Kunming City in Yunnan Province. Before utilization, all samples were filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove impurities. For use with real samples, the probe system was spiked with various concentrations of Cu2+ (0, 0.5, 5, 25 mg/L) using the standard addition method, and the fluorescence spectra were then recorded.

Two approaches (wet and dry) were utilized for the magnetic removal of Cu2+. In the wet method, the supernatant of M/CDs was mixed with 5 mg/L of Cu2+. After complete adsorption, the removal efficiency was measured and calculated by flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS). In the dry method, the powder of M/CDs (80 mg) was introduced into a solution containing 5 mg/L of Cu2+ in a volume of 20 mL. The mixture was transferred into a shaker and agitated at a speed of 250 rpm for a duration of 90 min. Then, utilizing a magnet for separation, the supernatant was attained to ascertain the removal efficiency of Cu2+ through FAAS. To investigate the reusability, the M/CDs were collected after the completion of adsorption and then fully dried. The M/CDs containing Cu2+ were submerged in a 50 mL solution of 0.1 M Na2CO3 for a duration of 6 h. The aforementioned procedure was replicated five times to study the reusability of the M/CDs. The calculation of the adsorption capacity and removal efficiency at equilibrium in the adsorption process was performed using the following equations [38]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, characterization, and stability of M/CDs

ce:section.ce:para: To design bifunctional materials with detection and adsorption, M/CDs were developed by hydrothermal method using FeCl3 and (−)-riboflavin as the source of iron and carbon, respectively (Fig. 1 a). The magnetism offered by Fe3O4 facilitated separation, and CDs provided a fluorescent sensing function. Additionally, the CDs and Fe3O4 were synthesized under identical conditions, allowing for a comprehensive comparison and evaluation of their fluorescent and magnetic properties. As can be seen from Fig. 1b, Fe3O4 almost did not exhibit fluorescence. Compared with CDs, M/CDs showed a more significant fluorescence response. Generally, the carbon core, surface, and dopants had a remarkable impact on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Accordingly, the fluorescence enhancement can be attributed to the introduction of multiple elements and intramolecular mixing under hydrothermal conditions [39]. This fluorescence enhancement mechanism was beneficial to improve the sensitivity of fluorescence detection and achieved a rapid response to the target identifier [30]. Notably, the saturation magnetization (Ms) value of M/CDs was 83.87 emu·g−1 (Fig. 1c) and it demonstrated the ability to be easily separated from aqueous solutions within 30 s by an external magnet (Fig. 1c, inset). These phenomena presented additional substantiation for the potential utilization of M/CDs for both separation and detection purposes.

To gain a deeper insight into the synthesis process of M/CDs, an XPS study was conducted to ascertain the surface element composition of both CDs and M/CDs. The XPS full spectrum scan results were shown in Fig. 1d, the main constituent elements of CDs were C, N, and O. After the composite with Fe3O4, the Fe 2p peak appeared in the spectrum, confirming the successful preparation of M/CDs. Analysis of the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) demonstrated that the C 1 s peak for CDs split into two distinct peaks: one representing C-C/C=C at 284.8 eV, comprising 71.17 % of the peak area, and another for C-N/C-O at 286.68 eV, which made up 28.83 % of the relative peak area [1,39]. In M/CDs, the relative peak area of C-N/C-O decreased to 17.75 %, indicating that the C-N/C-O groups of CDs participate in the binding with Fe3O4. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1f) revealed that the relative peak area of the N-H groups on the surface of M/CDs decreased from 43.84 % to 26.18 %, indicating that some of the N-H groups were consumed during the synthesis process of M/CDs [40]. The detailed O 1 s spectral analysis (Fig. 1g) revealed that the primary oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs’ surface were mainly C=O at 531.93 eV, with a relative peak area of 66.42 %, and C-O-C at 533.29 eV, occupying 33.58 % of the relative peak area. In the M/CDs, the C-O-C peak disappeared in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum, confirming that the C-O-C groups play a key role in the synthesis process. Additionally, a new peak appeared: Fe-O at 529.94 eV, with a relative peak area of 28.06 %, confirming the successful composite of Fe3O4 with M/CDs [41]. Upon deconvolution of the Fe 2p spectrum of M/CDs (Fig. 1h), six distinct peaks were identified. The peaks located at 709.6 eV and 722.7 eV corresponded to Fe (II) 2p3/2 and Fe (II) 2p1/2, respectively. Additionally, the peaks at 733.7 eV and 716 eV were associated with the satellite lines of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2. The remaining peaks at 712.4 eV and 725.9 eV were attributed to Fe (III) 2p3/2 and Fe (III) 2p1/2, respectively [35,42]. In light of the above results, a synthesis mechanism of M/CDs was proposed (Fig. 1i). The composite of CDs with Fe3O4 was primarily due to the strong affinity of C-N, N-H, and C-O-C bonds for Fe3O4.

To improve the fluorescence and magnetic properties of M/CDs, several preparation conditions such as the mole ratio of raw material (riboflavin / Fe3O4), temperature, and time were optimized. There was a discernable fluctuation in the fluorescence intensity as a result of altering the synthesis parameters. Based on the results presented in Fig. S2a-c, the highest fluorescent response and satisfactory magnetic performance of M/CDs used in further experiments were optimized as follows: the molar ratio of riboflavin / Fe3O4 was 6:5, at 200 °C, for 24 h.

The TEM technique was utilized to assess the surface morphologies of M/CDs. The TEM image showed that the CDs were almost spherical (Fig. S3a) and quite dispersed, with a lattice spacing of 2.9 Å (Fig. S3b) and an average size of approximately 4.8 nm (Fig. S3c). Based on the observation of Fig. 2 a, it can be noted that the prepared showed an approximately spherical shape with diameters of 5 ± 1.3 nm (Fig. 2c). Compared to CDs, its average size was slightly increased. Fig. 2b revealed that the surface of M/CDs exhibited remarkably parallel lattice fringes. The measurements and calculations were performed on the lattice fringes, resulting in the following findings: the lattice spacings of 2.5 Å and 2.9 Å were assigned to the (311) and (002) lattice planes of Fe3O4 and graphene, respectively [35,43]. Moreover, Fig. 2d exhibited the uniform distribution of C, N, O, and Fe elements on the M/CDs surface, confirming that CDs were successfully combined with Fe3O4. The composition of M/CDs was further confirmed by the corresponding energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) (Fig. 2e). The above results provide powerful proof to further confirm that M/CDs have been successfully prepared.

XRD measurement was performed to further explore the crystal structure of M/CDs. The characteristic peaks of CDs in Fig. 2f exhibited a wide diffraction peak at approximately 21.49°, which indicating a highly disordered carbon species [33,40]. The broad diffraction pattern additionally showcased that the synthesized M/CDs possessed a diminutive size, which aligns with the findings obtained from TEM analysis. The spikes of Fe3O4 at 2θ = 30.23°, 35.60°, 43.18°, 62.71°, and 74.14° correspond to the (220), (311), (400), (440), and (533) indices (PDF#11–0614) [44], respectively. Compared with Fe3O4, the crystallinity of M/CDs decreased, which could be attributed to the typically amorphous nature of CDs. It is noteworthy to mention that the diffraction peak of 35.60° shifted to 39.22° with the increase in the quantity of CDs. This discovery is consistent with prior reports that suggest certain diffraction peaks of Fe-based materials experience migration and disappearance after the introduction of additional particles [45]. Besides, the diffraction peak of CDs around 21.49° also shifted to 15.29°. Perhaps, the introductions of Fe3O4 could potentially influence the growth process of CDs, leading to alterations in the direction of crystal growth.

The changes in the surface functional groups of pure riboflavin, pure CDs, pure Fe3O4 and M/CDs were evaluated by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2g). The spectra of all four had a wide and strong peak appearing at 3440 cm−1, which belong to the stretching vibrational band of O-H [46]. A typical peak of Fe-O stretching vibration was observed at 578 cm−1 for M/CDs, indicating the presence of Fe3O4. Peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 of M/CDs belonged to the –CN and C=O groups [47]. The sharp peak at 2921 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H [48,49]. A strong peak at 1060 cm−1 related to the characteristic frequencies of the C-O group. As expected, the surface of M/CDs contained rich functional groups, which facilitated the separation and chelation of pollutants in water.

The photoluminescence properties of M/CDs were also measured. It is illustrated in Fig. 2h that the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of M/CDs exhibited a distinct absorption peak at 443 nm, which was attributed to the n → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system containing C=O [39]. According to the fluorescence spectrum, it was observed that the M/CDs displayed a peak emission at 560 nm when excited at 468 nm with the stokes shift of 92 nm. Furthermore, the emission spectra of M/CDs under different excitations in the range of 428–508 nm (Fig. 2i) was recorded. The emission wavelength of fluorescence of M/CDs almost unaffected by variations in the excitation wavelength, indicating that the M/CDs had excitation-independent properties. The M/CDs exhibited a quantum yield (QY) of 51.30 %, as calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (see Fig. S4). These findings highlighted the promising application of M/CDs as a fluorescent sensor.

The stability of fluorescent probes, including pH, salt tolerance, light stability, and storage stability, a critical consideration for their practical applications. As portrayed in Fig. S5a, it can be observed that M/CDs kept no variation with the pH value ranging from 4 to 12. This suggested that M/CDs remained in a relatively stable state in a wide pH range. It is clear from Fig. S5b that the addition of high concentrations of NaCl (1.0 M) did not result in any significant impact on the fluorescence signals, indicating the excellent salt tolerance of M/CDs. After irradiation with UV light (the power density = 24 W/m2, irradiation wavelength = 365 nm) for 1 h, the fluorescent responses of M/CDs displayed minimal alteration (Fig. S5c), illustrating an enormous photostability. As expected, M/CDs exhibited little impact on the fluorescent intensity after 7 days of storage at room temperature (Fig. S5d), indicating the advantageous optical stability of the M/CDs. All these findings implied that M/CDs possessed satisfactory optical stability, which is beneficial for their practical application.

The detection of Cu2+ by M/CDs was influenced by the incubated time and pH. Optimal detection performance by M/CDs was observed when the incubation time was set to 30 min and the pH was adjusted to 8.0 (Fig. S6). To validate the ability of M/CDs to quantify Cu2+, relevant fluorescence quenching experiments were performed with varying concentrations of Cu2+. The gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing the concentration of Cu2+ (Fig. 3 a). The relationship between ΔF and the concentration of Cu2+ was depicted in Fig. 3b, where ΔF showed a robust linear correlation with the Cu2+ concentration across the range of 0.2–50 mg/L. The linear regression equation was expressed as ΔF=9.025 CCu 2+ + 257.1 (inset of Fig. 3b), where ΔF represents the fluorescence quenching efficiency and CCu 2+ represents the concentration of Cu2+. The LOD was calculated to be 0.096 mg/L according to the following formula:
LOD =
3 σ
k
where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measures and k is the slope of calibration curve [50].

This method is superior to numerous methods that have been previously reported (Table S1). Furthermore, we applied the developed method to detect Cu2+ in actual water samples to evaluate its applicability in environmental analysis. From Table 1 , the average recovery of Cu2+ in different water samples ranged from 98.6 to 112 %, signifying that this method is dependable and holds promise for practical use.

To evaluate the selectivity and potential interference of the as-prepared M/CDs towards Cu2+, several ions that might coexist in sediment, such as Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cl-, HCO3 – and others, were individually introduced into the probe system for testing. As depicted in Fig. 3c, no obvious changes can be seen in fluorescent response with the addition of ions except Cu2+. Furthermore, when the aforementioned ions (at a concentration 5 times that of Cu2+) and Cu2+ were introduced simultaneously into the sensing probe system, there was no significant effect on the detection of Cu2+. Consequently, M/CDs exhibited high selectivity and excellent anti-interference properties towards Cu2+, which is appropriate as a fluorescence probe for practical analytical applications.

To further evaluate the adsorption properties of M/CDs, two removal experiments were conducted through both wet, and dry methods to investigate the Cu2+ removal efficiency. The removal efficiency of Cu2+ by Fe3O4, wet removal process of M/CDs and dry process of M/CDs were 0.77 %, 76.45 %, and 83.83 % (Fig. S7), respectively. The results showed that Fe3O4 was almost unable to remove Cu2+, while Cu2+ could be effectively removed by combining with CDs. Considering the more convenient operation of the dry process, the adsorption properties of the dry method were discussed in further detail in the following experiments. It is observed that as the dosage of M/CDs increased, the removal efficiency increased rapidly and then approached a stable value. The removal of Cu2+ by M/CDs could reach more than 80 % at an adsorbent dosage of 80 mg, and the related result was placed in the Fig. 4 a. Fig. 4b depicted that M/CDs had a satisfactory removal efficiency of 5 mg·L−1 Cu2+, owing to the rapid occupation of the adsorbent's active sites by Cu2+ at higher concentrations, leading to lower removal efficiency. The removal of M/CDs started with a rapid increase and then the increase gradually tended to be flat, finally reaching equilibrium at 90 min. This phenomenon occurs as a result of the gradual decrease in adsorption sites, and the adsorption efficiency gradually flattened over time (Fig. 4c). Fig. 4d illustrated that M/CDs can maintain a high removal efficiency for Cu2+ over a wide pH range (6–12). As presented in Fig. 4e, the presence of other coexisting anions and positive ions had no noticeably effect on the removal efficiency of Cu2+, indicating M/CDs had a favorable anti-interference performance in the removal of Cu2+.

To understand the adsorption process of M/CDs for Cu2+, adsorption experiments were conducted at various temperatures. Fig. S8 demonstrated that the adsorption capacity of M/CDs for Cu2+ increased with rising temperature, which can be initially inferred that the adsorption process was endothermic and that higher temperatures favor adsorption. Then, four kinetic adsorption models were utilized to analyze the adsorption performance (See Supporting Materials S1 and S2 for specific formulas). The plots of the fitted models and their corresponding parameters were displayed in Fig. S8 and detailed in Table S2, respectively. The comparison revealed that the adsorption fitting curve of M/CDs exhibited a higher degree of conformity with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The calculated values of adsorption capacity at equilibrium by pseudo-second-order kinetic fitting were 0.8077, 0.9729, and 1.198 mg·g−1, respectively, which closely matched the experimental values of 0.8087, 0.9611, and 1.173 mg·g−1. These results revealed that surface adsorption interactions played a crucial role in the rate control step [38,51]. Moreover, the adsorption process involved not only a simple contact between reactant molecules and the solid surface, but a complex process involving a variety of factors and mechanisms. Within this complex adsorption process, there may be a chemical adsorption process that played a dominant role [52]. The adsorption isotherm was the main elucidation of the interaction between the adsorbent and the target adsorption object. Based on the findings presented in Fig. S9 and Table S3, it can be concluded that the correlation coefficients of the Freundlich model (R2 = 0.9660–0.9789) could be better for fitting the experimental data. These results suggested that the removal process of Cu2+ can be elucidated through multilayer adsorption [53]. The value of 1/n was estimated to be in the range of 0.1 to 0.2, which indicated that adsorption is easy to occur [54,55]. The influence of temperature on the reaction is also significant. According to Table S4, the calculation of ΔH, ΔG, and ΔS indicated that the adsorption process was spontaneous, endothermic, and increasing disorderliness at the solid–liquid interface [56,57].

Adsorbents with good recycling properties are essential for practical applications. The several solutions/solvents were tried for desorption-regeneration of the adsorbent. Among them, a 0.1 M solution of Na2CO3 was a relatively effective eluent for eluting Cu2+ in the heavy metal ion-adsorbent system. The reutilization of M/CDs for Cu2+ adsorption was illustrated in Fig. 4f. The results showed that the removal efficiency for Cu2+ of the heavy metal ion-adsorbent system decreased from 89.54 % to 76.92 % with the increase of reuse cycles. The M/CDs can be reused for at least five cycles with a regeneration loss of about 12.62 %, which indicates that the adsorbent has good reusability for practical applications.

The fluorescence performance of M/CDs were mainly provided by CDs, and the combination of CDs and Fe3O4 further enhanced the fluorescence response via the fluorescence enhancement mechanism. While the presence of Cu2+ significantly decreased the fluorescence response of M/CDs. In general, the theories to elucidate the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching usually include dynamic quenching and static quenching [58]. In investigating the mechanism of M/CDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+, a series of experiments were conducted to gain a deeper understanding of the fluorescence quenching process. Firstly, UV–visible spectroscopy was utilized to examine M/CDs and their mixtures with differing concentrations of Cu2+, the results were presented in Fig. 5 a. The absorption band around 445 nm for M/CDs exhibited negligible alterations in intensity and wavelength, both before to and after the introduction of Cu2+, suggesting the absence of complex formation between Cu2+ and M/CDs. Therefore, it was preliminarily inferred that the fluorescence quenching of M/CDs induced by Cu2+ was a dynamic quenching process [59]. To gain a more in-depth understanding of the mechanism of this fluorescence quenching, the effect of Cu2+ on the fluorescence lifetime of M/CDs was investigated. The fluorescence decay curves of M/CDs with and without Cu2+ were fitted using a bi-exponential function:

[60]. Fig. 5b illustrated that without Cu2+, the M/CDs exhibited an average fluorescence lifetime of 6.37 ns. With the introduction of Cu2+, the fluorescence lifetime dropped to 5.43 ns, providing additional evidence that the quenching process was of the dynamic type. This dynamic quenching process may involve Cu2+ the formation of coordination bonds between Cu2+ and functional groups on the surface of M/CDs, such as amino or hydroxyl groups. This interaction could trigger an effective photo-induced electron transfer process, which promoted non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes, leading to the annihilation of excitons and resulting in the fluorescence quenching of M/CDs [61].

To delve deeper into the adsorption process, XPS analysis was employed to examine the adsorbent’s properties before and after adsorption. It can be observed from Fig. 5c that a new Cu 2p peak appeared in the spectrum after adsorption, indicating that the target analyte Cu2+ had been successfully adsorbed by M/CDs. The C 1 s spectrum of M/CDs was deconvoluted into two distinct peaks, corresponding to C-C/C=C at 284.8 eV and C-N/C-O at 286.6 eV, respectively [35]. A comparison of the high-resolution C 1 s spectra before and after adsorption showed a shift towards lower binding energy for the C-N/C-O peak, indicating that the C-N/C-O functional groups in the adsorbent were instrumental in the adsorption mechanism (Fig. 5d). Fig. 5e displayed the high-resolution N 1 s spectra of M/CDs before to and after Cu2+ adsorption. The fitted N 1 s peaks at 400.5 eV and 399.4 eV were associated with N-H and N-C bonds, respectively. These bonds also shifted to lower binding energies after adsorption, signifying that the N atoms of M/CDs as active sites for Cu2+ adsorption [62]. The shift of the C=O bond to a lower binding energy after adsorption indicated that Cu2+ interacted with the oxygen-containing functional groups on the adsorbent (Fig. 5f) [63]. These findings indicated that the C-N/C-O, N-H, N-C and C=O species were most likely to be involved in the chemical coordination with Cu2+.

A novel bifunctional, low-cost material (M/CDs) had been successfully prepared by an eco-friendly and simple approach for rapid fluorescent detection and efficient adsorptive removal of Cu2+. The prepared M/CDs exhibited small size, abundant functional groups, favorable magnetic behavior, excellent fluorescence properties, and satisfactory stability. As a fluorescence probe, Cu2+ significantly quenched the fluorescence signal of M/CDs due to the energy resonance transfer. Also, the M/CDs had a broad response range, low detection limit, and good anti-interference to the detection of Cu2+. As a green adsorbent, the complexation of CDs made the adsorbent surface with abundant functional groups (such as C-H, C=O, C-N and C-O), which was the main reason for the high removal rate of Cu2+ by M/CD. And the adsorption process could be well described by pseudo-second-order and Freundlich model. The process of adsorption was spontaneous, endothermic, and feasible. More notably, even after five cycles, the M/CDs still exhibited a high removal efficiency of Cu2+. Moreover, the superparamagnetic nature of M/CDs made it easily separated after the adsorption process through a magnet, which facilitates reuse. Overall, the as-prepared M/CDs exhibited a versatile strategy for the rapid sensitive detection and magnetic removal of metal ions in wastewater. Being an emerging force in the field of green adsorbent materials, this may promote the advancement of multifunctional probes for water remediation.
